commands.repair_reason: - [P2] The remaining action is maintainer acceptance of the compatibility-sensitive naming migration and stacked landing order, not a narrow automated repair.; Cleared: The diff touches QMD memory runtime, docs, and tests only; no CI, dependency, package, secret, permission, or supply-chain surface change was found. (sha=689960f12f08104fd218c3f9b604845b7d354be9)
commands.review_summary:
The PR changes QMD managed collection names from agent-suffixed identifiers to unscoped base names, adds scoped-to-unscoped migration/rebind handling, and updates memory docs and tests.

PR surface: Source +23, Tests +465, Docs 0. Total +488 across 8 files.

Reproducibility: not applicable. This PR is a compatibility-sensitive refactor rather than a standalone bug report. The upgrade behavior is source-provable from the migration code and covered by PR-branch regression tests plus supplied real terminal proof.
commands.review_followup: - [P2] The remaining action is maintainer acceptance of the compatibility-sensitive naming migration and stacked landing order, not a narrow automated repair.

target.title: refactor(memory): drop redundant agent-id scoping from qmd collection names
target.body:
## Summary

- **What:** drop the redundant `-<agentId>` scoping from managed qmd collection names. Each agent has its **own isolated qmd index** (`agents/<agentId>/qmd/index.sqlite`), so collection names can't collide across agents â€” the agent-id suffix adds nothing but hardcodes the agent id into collection identifiers.
- **How:** remove `scopeCollectionBase` (and two now-dead path helpers, `isPathInsideRoot` / `canonicalizePathForContainment`); name default collections by their base (`memory-root`, `memory-dir`), the session collection `sessions`, and extra collections by their explicit name. Reverse the legacy migration to **scopedâ†’unscoped** (`migrateLegacyScopedCollections` / `deriveLegacyScopedName`) so existing per-agent indexes migrate cleanly.
- **Why migration is cheap:** qmd collections are a **rebuildable derived index** (not user data), so the migration drops the stale scoped collection and lets the unscoped one be (re)created + reindexed â€” no import, no data loss (consistent with this repo's "prefer delete/drop/rebuild over import for cache").
- **Outcome:** stable, agent-agnostic collection names; net **âˆ’56 LOC** for the de-scope itself (removes the scoping helper + a path-containment branch).
- **Orphan cleanup:** the scopedâ†’unscoped migration is **kind-gated**. Engine-owned collections (`memory`/`sessions`, whose base names are engine-generated and not user-suppliable) are removed even when their pattern has drifted from the current default; user-named (`custom`) collections keep the path+pattern guards and are **never** auto-deleted. Without this, a pattern-drifted legacy engine collection lingered as an orphan and was re-walked + re-embedded by every `qmd update` â€” a recurring reindex cost the migration otherwise shipped. (Trade-off, stated for transparency: for engine kinds this intentionally drops the prior "a user coincidentally named a collection like a legacy scoped name" guard â€” safe because those base names cannot be user-supplied; `custom` retains it.)
- **Stacked on #91253 (per ClawSweeper):** the first commit is the path-aware qmd-rebind fix (#91253); the second is the de-scope. The rebind is **required** for the migration to be safe â€” without path-aware metadata, an upgraded index holding both a stale *unscoped* collection (old path) and a current *scoped* one could keep the stale unscoped target active; with the rebind folded in, the stale unscoped collection is rebound to the current path. Once #91253 lands, this rebases to just the de-scope commit.

## Linked context

Closes #91259

Depends on / folds in #91253 (path-aware qmd collection rebind) â€” the first commit of this PR.

Related: #91265 (an earlier PR toward the same proposal; this one folds in the path-aware rebind so existing scoped indexes migrate safely, adds an upgrade-case regression, and includes live before/after behavior proof).

Requested by a maintainer or owner? No â€” follows from the proposal in #91259.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

Behavior addressed: managed qmd collection names dropping the redundant `-<agentId>` suffix, with existing scoped per-agent indexes migrating to the unscoped names.

Real environment tested: a live, self-hosted OpenClaw gateway (macOS), memory backend `qmd` (`@tobilu/qmd` 2.5.2), running this build, against an agent whose index already held scoped collections.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```
# build the gateway with this change, restart, then force a reindex of an agent
# whose qmd index already holds scoped collections:
openclaw gateway restart
openclaw memory index --force --agent <agent>
sqlite3 <agent-qmd-index>.sqlite "select name,path from store_collections order by name;"
```

Evidence after fix (live gateway, identifiers redacted):
```
# BEFORE (existing index, scoped names):
memory-root-<agent>   <WS>
memory-dir-<agent>    <WS>/memory
sessions-<agent>      <agent-state>/qmd/sessions
team-library-<agent>  <WS>/library/promoted
team-library          <WS-old>/library/promoted   # orphan duplicate

# AFTER (this patch, reindex):
memory-root    <WS>
memory-dir     <WS>/memory
sessions       <agent-state>/qmd/sessions
team-library   <WS>/library/promoted
# scoped (-<agent>) collections remaining: 0
```

Observed result after fix: all managed collections migrated to unscoped names; `0` scoped collections remained; the prior orphan duplicate (`team-library` + `team-library-<agent>`) collapsed to a single `team-library`.

What was not tested: a fresh (never-scoped) index â€” covered by unit tests (default + custom collection names assert the unscoped form); and sandbox/container-mode end-to-end.

Proof limitations or environment constraints: live evidence is redacted terminal output from a private fleet (paths/agent/team identifiers removed).

Before evidence: shown inline above (the pre-reindex scoped names + orphan duplicate).

### Kind-gated orphan cleanup â€” local proof (real `qmd` 2.5.2 binary + real engine)

A scratch per-agent index was seeded with two legacy scoped collections, both with a **drifted pattern** (`**/*.txt`, vs the engine's `**/*.md`) â€” the live trigger, since `qmd 2.5.2 collection list` emits no path so the path guard is dead. One is engine-owned (`memory` kind), one user-owned (`custom` kind). The real `QmdMemoryManager` then runs its migration (cli mode â€” no embedding):
```
BEFORE (seeded orphans):
  memory-dir-proofagent   Pattern: **/*.txt     # engine-owned (memory)
  notes-proofagent        Pattern: **/*.txt     # user-owned   (custom)

AFTER (real engine ran, mode=cli):
  notes-proofagent        Pattern: **/*.txt     # LEFT  â€” user data never auto-deleted
  memory-root / memory-dir / notes              # unscoped, engine-created
  (memory-dir-proofagent REMOVED)

  memory-dir-proofagent : 1 â†’ 0   (engine-owned, removed)
  notes-proofagent      : 1 â†’ 1   (user-owned, preserved)
```
Both orphans had a drifted pattern â€” the exact case the old guard skipped for both. The kind-gate removes the engine-owned one and still protects the user-owned one, proven end-to-end with the real binary.

## Tests and validation

Commands run (clean checkout off `openclaw/openclaw` `main`):
- `pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/memory` â†’ **436 passed (40 files)**
- `pnpm test packages/memory-host-sdk` â†’ **38 passed (9 files)**
- `pnpm check:test-types` (tsgo) â†’ **clean, 0 errors**
- `oxfmt` applied to the touched files.

Regression coverage:
- Default + custom collection-name tests now assert the unscoped form.
- The unscopedâ†’scoped migration tests were replaced with scopedâ†’unscoped ones, plus a migration-guard regression (a scoped collection whose path does not match the desired collection is skipped, not clobbered).
- **Upgrade-case regression (addresses the flagged compatibility risk):** an index holding BOTH a stale *unscoped* collection (old path) AND a current *scoped* one at the name switch â†’ asserts the stale unscoped collection is rebound to the **current** path (via the folded-in `collection show` path enrichment), not left active.
- **Kind-gated orphan cleanup:** a rewritten migration-guard test â€” a `memory`-kind legacy scoped collection with a drifted pattern is now **removed** (genuinely fails against the pre-fix guard), while a `custom`-kind one with a drifted pattern is **left** â€” plus a contract test asserting every managed collection carries a `kind` (the safety gate depends on it).

Multi-agent note: safe by construction â€” each agent has its own isolated qmd index, so unscoped names cannot collide across agents.

## Risk checklist

User-visible behavior change? Internal only â€” qmd collection identifiers (a derived index) change; no user-facing API/config change.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? Migration only: existing per-agent indexes migrate scopedâ†’unscoped on the next `ensureCollections()` (drop stale scoped + recreate unscoped + reindex). No config keys changed.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? No.

Highest-risk area: the scopedâ†’unscoped migration on existing indexes, and the kind-gated removal of legacy scoped collections.

How is that risk mitigated: removal only ever targets the exact derived legacy name `<base>-<agentId>` for a **desired** collection. For `custom` collections the path+pattern guards remain, so a user-named collection is never auto-deleted. For `memory`/`sessions` the base names are engine-generated and cannot be user-supplied, so the derived legacy name is unambiguously engine-owned; if such a collection is still configured it is simply re-added at the same path/pattern (net-zero, a reindex). qmd collections are rebuildable, so any dropped-then-recreated collection reindexes from source with no user-visible data loss.

## Current review state

Next action: maintainer review.

What is still waiting: CI + review.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed: none (initial submission).
target.author: sasan1200
labels: status: ðŸ‘€ ready for maintainer look
